title: Regularity theorem for totally nonnegative flag varieties
overall_summary: The paper proves that Lusztig and Rietsch's cell decomposition of the totally nonnegative part of any partial flag variety G/P is a regular CW complex, so every closed cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball. The proof is split into an abstract topological criterion and a geometric construction: a shellable totally nonnegative space equipped with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas has conical product neighborhoods of all cells, and hence its links and closed cells are balls by induction, shellability, and the generalized Poincare conjecture. The atlas for G/P is built by embedding local affine charts of projected Richardson strata into affine flag varieties, where Bruhat/Gaussian decomposition gives product coordinates and a torus action gives radial contraction. For the original matching-complex problem, the most useful contribution is the abstract link-induction package: if the matching complex can be given a shellable/thin face poset and local star charts of the form cell times cone on link, the paper gives a robust route to regularity, ball closures, and contractibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a semisimple algebraic group split over R, let P be a parabolic subgroup, and let (G/P)_{≥0} be Lusztig's totally nonnegative part of the partial flag variety G/P. Rietsch's decomposition of (G/P)_{≥0} into totally positive cells is a regular CW complex. Consequently, the closure of each cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is obtained by combining the abstract regularity criterion, Theorem 2.4, with the geometric verification in Theorem 2.5. The known results of Rietsch, Williams, and Knutson-Lam-Speyer show that (G/P, (G/P)_{≥0}, Q_J) is a shellable totally nonnegative space: the closure poset Q_J is graded, thin, and shellable; the strata are smooth projected Richardson varieties; positive pieces are open balls; and positive closure relations match the poset. The main new geometric work constructs a Fomin-Shapiro atlas for this space by embedding suitable affine charts in G/P into affine flag varieties. Once this atlas exists, the purely topological link-induction argument proves regularity and ball closures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Grassmannian Gr(k,n), the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr_{≥0}(k,n), stratified by Postnikov's positroid cells according to which Plucker coordinates vanish and which are positive, is a regular CW complex. The closure of every positroid cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the special case of Theorem 1.1 in which G/P is Gr(k,n). The totally nonnegative part in Lusztig's sense agrees with Postnikov's Gr_{≥0}(k,n), and Rietsch's cell decomposition agrees with the positroid cell decomposition. Thus the regularity and ball-closure conclusion follows directly from Theorem 1.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4 (abstract regularity criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) be a shellable totally nonnegative space in the following precise sense. The finite poset Q, after adjoining a minimum element 0-hat, is graded, thin, shellable, and has a unique maximal element 1-hat; Y is a smooth manifold stratified as Y = disjoint union over g in Q of smooth embedded strata Y_g^o, with dim Y_g^o equal to the rank dim(g) and closure Y_h = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^o; Y_{≥0} is compact; for each g, Y_g^{>0} = Y_g^o ∩ Y_{≥0} is a connected component of Y_g^o diffeomorphic to R_{>0}^{dim(g)}; and the closure of Y_h^{>0} inside Y is Y_h^{≥0} = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^{>0}. Assume further that (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) admits a Fomin-Shapiro atlas: for every g in Q, with Star_g = union over h ⪰ g of Y_h^o, there is an open set O_g ⊂ Star_g, a smooth cone (Z_g, θ_g) with Z_g a closed embedded submanifold of R^{N_g} and θ_g an R_{>0}-action increasing Euclidean norm away from 0, and a diffeomorphism ν_g: O_g → (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × Z_g such that strata become products, the stratum Y_g is sent to (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × {0}, the cone action preserves the pieces of Z_g, and the positive star Star_g^{≥0} is contained in O_g. Then the decomposition Y_{≥0} = disjoint union over h in Q of Y_h^{>0} is a regular CW complex. In particular, every Y_h^{≥0} is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension dim(h).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on cell dimension, relying on a nested induction for links. For each pair g ≺ h, Section 3 defines the link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} as the intersection of the normal positive piece Z_{g,h}^{≥0} with the unit sphere in R^{N_g}. Lemma 3.2 proves that link strata are contractible smooth manifolds and that the closed links are compact. Proposition 3.5 identifies the positive star Star_{g,h}^{≥0} with Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}). Lemma 3.6 gives local models for points in lower-dimensional link strata in terms of smaller links. Theorem 3.12 then proves by induction that every link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is a closed ball. Returning to Y_h^{≥0}, a point in the interior cell Y_h^{>0} has Euclidean local coordinates, while a point in a lower cell Y_g^{>0}, g ≺ h, has a neighborhood Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), hence a half-space local model because the link is a ball. Thus Y_h^{≥0} is a compact topological manifold with boundary equal to the union of lower positive cells. Its interior Y_h^{>0} is contractible, so by Brown collaring the whole manifold is contractible. By induction the boundary is a regular CW complex with face poset the open interval (0-hat,h); shellability and thinness imply this boundary is a sphere by Theorem 3.9. The generalized Poincare consequence Theorem 3.10 then implies Y_h^{≥0} is a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every partial flag variety G/P of a simple simply connected algebraic group G split over R, the triple ((G/P)_R, (G/P)_{≥0}, Q_J), where Q_J is Rietsch's closure poset of projected Richardson cells, is a shellable totally nonnegative space and admits a Fomin-Shapiro atlas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The shellable totally nonnegative axioms are verified using known results: Williams proves that the poset Q_J with an added minimum is graded, thin, and shellable; Knutson-Lam-Speyer and related Richardson-variety theory give smooth projected Richardson strata and their closure relations; Rietsch proves the positive cells, their positivity components, and their closure relations; compactness is immediate from compactness of the real partial flag variety. For the Fomin-Shapiro atlas, fix a minimal element f = (u,u) below g in Q_J. The paper constructs an affine Bruhat atlas map φ_u from an affine chart C_u^{(J)} in G/P to the affine flag variety G/B. The map sends projected Richardson strata in the star of f isomorphically to affine Richardson strata. The affine flag variety has Gaussian/Bruhat product projections ν_g around the big cell C_{ψ(g)}, and a dominant affine torus action supplies the smooth cone structure. The open set O_g is the preimage of C_{ψ(g)} under φ_u; Theorem 7.3, whose positivity part relies on the subtraction-free analysis of Sections 5 and 6, proves that O_g contains the whole positive star Star_g^{≥0}. These data satisfy all Fomin-Shapiro axioms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) is a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. Fix g ≺ h in Q. Write ν_g = (ν_{g,1}, ν_{g,2}). Define Star_{g,h}^{≥0} = Y_h^{≥0} ∩ Star_g^{≥0}; define Z_g^{≥0} = ν_{g,2}(Star_g^{≥0}), Z_{g,h}^{≥0} = ν_{g,2}(Star_{g,h}^{≥0}), Z_{g,h}^{>0} = Z_g^{≥0} ∩ Z_{g,h}^o, where Z_{g,h}^o is the stratum in Z_g corresponding to h; let S_g be the unit sphere in R^{N_g}; and define Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} = Z_{g,h}^{≥0} ∩ S_g and Lk_{g,h}^{>0} = Z_{g,h}^{>0} ∩ S_g. Then: (i) for x in O_g, x lies in Y_h^{>0} if and only if ν_g(x) lies in Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}; (ii) Z_{g,h}^{>0} is an embedded submanifold of Z_g of dimension dim(h) - dim(g), intersects S_g transversely, and is preserved by the cone action θ_g(t,−) for all t > 0; (iii) Lk_{g,h}^{>0} is a contractible smooth manifold of dimension dim(h) - dim(g) - 1; and (iv) Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), the positive star Star_g^{≥0} is connected because it contains the dense top positive cell. Its image under ν_{g,1} is connected, lies in Y_g^o ∩ O_g, and contains Y_g^{>0}; since Y_g^{>0} is a connected component, the image is Y_g^{>0}. Applying the product-stratification axiom shows that ν_g(Y_h^{>0}) is contained in Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}. The set Z_{g,h}^{>0} is then a connected subset of the corresponding Z-stratum; taking the connected component C containing it, ν_g^{-1}(Y_g^{>0} × C) is a connected subset of Y_h^o ∩ O_g containing Y_h^{>0}, so by the positive-component axiom it equals Y_h^{>0}. For (ii), since Y_h^{>0} is an embedded submanifold and ν_g(Y_h^{>0}) = Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}, Lemma 3.3 implies Z_{g,h}^{>0} is embedded, with the dimension difference. The cone action preserves the ambient stratum and hence the connected component Z_{g,h}^{>0}. Strict increase of norm along action orbits implies transversality with the unit sphere. For (iii), the product Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible because it is homeomorphic to Y_h^{>0}; hence Z_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible as a retract. The cone action radially retracts Z_{g,h}^{>0} onto its unit-sphere slice Lk_{g,h}^{>0}. For (iv), Star_{g,h}^{≥0} = Y_h^{≥0} ∩ O_g is closed in O_g, so its image is closed in Y_g^{>0} × Z_g; projecting gives Z_{g,h}^{≥0} closed in Z_g. Intersecting with the unit sphere in finite-dimensional R^{N_g} gives compactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be smooth manifolds. If B' is a subset of B such that A × B' is an embedded submanifold of A × B, then B' is an embedded submanifold of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose any point a in A. The projection A × B' → A has a as a regular value, so {a} × B' is an embedded submanifold of A × B'. Since A × B' is embedded in A × B, the slice {a} × B' is embedded in {a} × B, which identifies B' as an embedded submanifold of B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let θ: R_{>0} × R^N → R^N be a smooth map giving an R_{>0}-action on R^N such that ∂/∂t ||θ(t,x)|| > 0 for every t > 0 and every nonzero x. Then: (i) θ(t,0) = 0 for all t > 0; (ii) lim_{t→0+} θ(t,x) = 0 for all x in R^N; and (iii) for every nonzero x there is a unique t_1(x) > 0 such that ||θ(t_1(x),x)|| = 1, and the function t_1: R^N \ {0} → R_{>0} is continuous.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper reduces this to properties of contractive flows from earlier work by setting f(s,x) = θ(e^{-s},x). The strict monotonicity of norm along θ-orbits becomes strict contraction for f as s increases. Such flows fix the origin and contract every point to the origin in the limit t → 0+. Strict monotonicity gives uniqueness of the time at which an orbit meets the unit sphere, and standard implicit/flow-continuity arguments give continuity of t_1. Compactness of the unit sphere is used later to obtain uniform convergence near the cone point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas, for any g ≺ h in Q: (i) the set Z_{g,h}^{≥0} is homeomorphic to the open cone Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), with 0 mapping to the cone point and each stratum Z_{g,g'}^{>0}, g ≺ g' ⪯ h, mapping to Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} × R_{>0}; and (ii) the positive star Star_{g,h}^{≥0} is homeomorphic to Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), with Y_g^{>0} mapping to Y_g^{>0} times the cone point.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For nonzero x in Z_{g,h}^{≥0}, let t_1(x) be the unique scaling time from Lemma 3.4 such that ||θ_g(t_1(x),x)|| = 1. Define ξ(0) to be the cone point and ξ(x) = (θ_g(t_1(x),x), 1/t_1(x)) for x ≠ 0. The inverse sends the cone point to 0 and sends (y,t) in Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} × R_{>0} to θ_g(t,y). Continuity away from the cone point is immediate. Continuity at 0 follows because x_n → 0 forces t_1(x_n) → ∞, otherwise monotonicity would keep θ_g(R,x_n) at norm at least 1 for a bounded R and contradict θ_g(R,0)=0. Continuity of the inverse at the cone point follows from uniform convergence θ_g(t,y) → 0 for y on the compact unit sphere as t → 0+. Part (ii) combines part (i) with Lemma 3.2(i), which identifies Star_{g,h}^{≥0} under ν_g with Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{≥0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. Let g ≺ p ⪯ h in Q, let x_p be a point of the link stratum Lk_{g,p}^{>0}, and put d = dim(p) - dim(g) - 1. Then there is a local homeomorphism from (Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}, x_p) to (Z_{p,h}^{≥0} × R^d, (0,0)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a point x_g in Y_g^{>0}. On the open subset H_p of Z_g consisting of points x such that ν_g^{-1}(x_g,x) lies in O_p, define θ_{g,p}(x) = ν_{p,2}(ν_g^{-1}(x_g,x)) for x in H_p ∩ S_g. The point x_p lies in H_p and maps to 0. The key is to show x_p is a regular point of θ_{g,p}. The differential of ν_{p,2} has kernel the tangent space to Y_p^o, and the slice ν_g^{-1}({x_g} × (H_p ∩ S_g)) meets Y_p^{>0} transversely because Lemma 3.2 gives transversality of Z_{g,p}^{>0} with S_g. The submersion theorem then gives local coordinates in which θ_{g,p} is projection onto the Z_p-coordinate, with an R^d fiber. Finally, by comparing positivity under the two product charts, a nearby point x lies in Lk_{g,r}^{>0} exactly when θ_{g,p}(x) lies in Z_{p,r}^{>0}. Taking the union over p ⪯ r ⪯ h identifies the local piece of Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} with (local piece of Z_{p,h}^{≥0}) × R^d.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a graded finite poset is shellable, then every interval in that poset is shellable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Bjorner's result. Shellability of the order complex restricts to intervals because the order complex of an interval is a link or induced subcomplex controlled by the shelling order. This is used repeatedly so that every boundary face poset appearing in the induction is shellable, not only the global poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a regular CW complex with face poset Q. If Q with an added minimum element 0-hat and maximum element 1-hat is graded, thin, and shellable, then X is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(Q) - 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites results of Lundell-Weingram, Danaraj-Klee, and Bjorner. Thinness and shellability imply that the order complex of the proper part of the augmented face poset is a shellable pseudomanifold with sphere topology. For regular CW complexes, the barycentric subdivision is precisely the order complex of the face poset, so the topology of X is recovered from the poset. This theorem is the mechanism converting shellable boundary posets into actual topological spheres in the link induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a compact contractible n-dimensional topological manifold with boundary. If its boundary ∂C is homeomorphic to an (n-1)-sphere, then C is homeomorphic to an n-dimensional closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a standard consequence of the generalized Poincare conjecture of Smale, Freedman, and Perelman. The proof sketch given in the paper is: Brown's collar theorem gives a collar of ∂C in C. Attach an n-ball along the collared boundary sphere to form a closed topological manifold C'. Van Kampen gives simple connectivity, and Mayer-Vietoris gives that C' is a homology sphere. The generalized Poincare conjecture implies C' is a sphere. Brown's Schoenflies theorem then implies that the original C is a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If C is a topological manifold with boundary ∂C, then C is homotopy equivalent to its interior C \ ∂C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is another consequence of Brown's collar theorem. A collar identifies a neighborhood of ∂C with ∂C × [0,1), and pushing the boundary slightly into the collar gives a deformation of C onto the open manifold obtained by removing the boundary. In the paper this is used to prove a compact manifold closure is contractible once its interior cell is known to be contractible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.12 (link induction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) be a shellable totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. For every g ≺ h in Q, the link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} = disjoint union over g ≺ g' ⪯ h of Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} is a regular CW complex homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension dim(h) - dim(g) - 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on d = dim(h) - dim(g) - 1. For d = 0, Lemma 3.2 says the only link stratum is a contractible 0-manifold, hence a point. For d > 0, first prove Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is a topological manifold with boundary equal to the union of lower link strata Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} for g ≺ g' ≺ h. At a point in the top stratum Lk_{g,h}^{>0}, Lemma 3.2 gives ordinary manifold coordinates. At a point in a lower stratum Lk_{g,g'}^{>0}, Lemma 3.6 gives a local model Z_{g',h}^{≥0} × R^{d'}, and Proposition 3.5 plus the induction hypothesis identify Z_{g',h}^{≥0} with a cone on a ball, hence a half-space. Thus the stated boundary formula holds. Lemma 3.2 gives compactness. The interior Lk_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible by Lemma 3.2, and Proposition 3.11 implies the compact manifold is contractible. By induction, the boundary is a regular CW complex whose face poset is the open interval (g,h). The interval [g,h] is graded, thin, and shellable because the global augmented poset is and intervals inherit shellability, so Theorem 3.9 identifies the boundary with a sphere. Theorem 3.10 then identifies the link with a closed ball. The CW axioms follow from the boundary decomposition and induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proof of Theorem 2.4 (cell-closure induction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 2.4, for each h in Q the closed positive cell Y_h^{≥0} = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^{>0} is a compact topological manifold with boundary union over g ≺ h of Y_g^{>0}, and it is a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proceed by induction on dim(h). If dim(h)=0, Y_h^{>0} is a point. For dim(h)>0, let x lie in Y_h^{≥0}. If x is in the open cell Y_h^{>0}, then the positive cell is locally Euclidean. If x lies in a lower cell Y_g^{>0}, then Star_g^{≥0} is open in Y_{≥0}; Proposition 3.5 gives a neighborhood Star_{g,h}^{≥0} homeomorphic to Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}); Theorem 3.12 says the link is a closed ball, so this is a half-space local model. Therefore Y_h^{≥0} is a compact manifold with boundary the lower-cell union. Its interior is Y_h^{>0}, which is contractible, and Proposition 3.11 gives contractibility of the closure. The boundary is regular by induction, and its face poset is the interval (0-hat,h) in the augmented poset, hence graded, thin, and shellable. Theorem 3.9 makes the boundary a sphere, and Theorem 3.10 makes Y_h^{≥0} a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.2 (Bruhat product projection)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the affine flag variety G/B with Iwahori subgroups B and B_- and unipotent radicals U and U_-, fix an affine Weyl group element g. Let C_g = dot{g} B_- B/B, U_1(g) = dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} ∩ U, and U_2(g) = dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} ∩ U_-. Then: (i) for every xB in C_g there are unique y_1 in U_1(g) and y_2 in U_2(g) such that y_1 xB lies in the opposite cell through g and y_2 xB lies in the Schubert cell through g; (ii) the resulting map ν_tilde_g: C_g → opposite cell at g × Schubert cell at g is a biregular isomorphism; and (iii) for h ≤ g ≤ f in the affine Bruhat order, ν_tilde_g restricts to a biregular isomorphism C_g ∩ R_h^f → R_g^f × R_h^g between the corresponding open Richardson pieces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key algebraic input is that multiplication gives two biregular factorizations U_1(g) × U_2(g) → dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} and U_2(g) × U_1(g) → dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1}. Given x in dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1}, one can solve y_1 x in U_2(g) and y_2 x in U_1(g) uniquely by comparing the two factorizations. The inverse map is explicit using Gaussian decomposition: for x_1 in U_1(g) and x_2 in U_2(g), find y_1,y_2 with y_1 x_2 = y_2 x_1 by setting y_2 = [x_1 x_2^{-1}]_-^{-1} and y_1 = [x_1 x_2^{-1}]_+ in the big cell decomposition. Regularity follows from regularity of Gaussian factors. Finally, multiplying by U_1(g) does not change the upper Bruhat index and multiplying by U_2(g) does not change the lower Bruhat index in the relevant way; thus if xB lies in the Richardson stratum R_h^f, the two projected factors lie in R_g^f and R_h^g, and the product decomposition gives the stated isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3 (affine Bruhat atlas for projected Richardson strata)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G/P be a partial flag variety, let Q_J be the projected Richardson closure poset, and choose an antidominant coweight λ whose stabilizer in the Weyl group is W_J. Define ψ: Q_J → affine Weyl group by ψ(v,w) = v τ_λ w^{-1}. For u in W^J, let C_u^{(J)} be the standard affine chart in G/P around uP, and let φ_bar_u: C_u^{(J)} → G/B be the map constructed from the two Bruhat projections of the chart coordinates. Then: (1) φ_bar_u is a biregular isomorphism from C_u^{(J)} onto X_{τ_λ^J} ∩ Schubert cell τ_{uλ}, and for every (v,w) ⪰ (u,u) in Q_J it restricts to a biregular isomorphism C_u^{(J)} ∩ Π_{v,w}^o → affine Richardson cell R_{vτ_λw^{-1}}^{τ_{uλ}}; (2) if (u,u) ⪯ (v,w) ⪯ (v',w') in Q_J, then φ_bar_u sends the positive cell Π_{v',w'}^{>0} into the affine big cell C_{vτ_λw^{-1}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), write a point in C_u^{(J)} using the group dot{u} U_-^{(J)} dot{u}^{-1}. A finite-dimensional Bruhat projection splits this element into U_1^{(J)} and U_2^{(J)} parts, and φ_bar_u is represented by g_1^{(J)} dot{τ}_{uλ} (g_2^{(J)})^{-1}. Conjugation identities for dot{τ}_λ show this lies in the affine Schubert cell through τ_{uλ} and in the correct opposite Schubert varieties. The image is shown to be a closed subvariety of the affine Schubert cell of the same dimension as X_{τ_λ^J} ∩ Schubert cell τ_{uλ}; irreducibility of Richardson varieties then gives equality. The stratum-level statement follows from checking that projected Richardson strata map into the corresponding affine Richardson strata and using dimension/isomorphism. For (2), one must prove the positive star is contained in the domain C_{ψ(v,w)}. This is the technically hardest part of the paper: Sections 5 and 6 build subtraction-free Marsh-Rietsch parametrizations and prove nonvanishing of certain generalized minors for positive points. Theorem 6.4 shows that the required Gaussian decomposition exists on all positive cells above (v,w), and translating this condition through the formula for φ_bar_u gives membership in C_{vτ_λw^{-1}}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (u,u) ⪯ (v,w) ⪯ (v',w') in Q_J, and let x be an element of G such that xB lies in the totally positive Richardson cell R_{v',w'}^{>0}. The paper defines, on a suitable domain G_{u,v}^{(J)}, a regular map ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}: G_{u,v}^{(J)} → G using Bruhat projection κ, parabolic Gaussian projection π_{dot{u}P_-}, and a Levi factor η. Then x lies in G_{u,v}^{(J)}, and ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) lies in B B_- dot{w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is proved via a subtraction-free argument. First, Section 5 develops subtraction-free versions of Marsh-Rietsch parametrizations of positive Richardson cells, proving that certain coordinate changes and Bruhat collision moves preserve subtraction-free rationality. These imply that generalized minors Δ_i^{±} evaluated on relevant expressions are nonnegative subtraction-free functions and are strictly positive when forced by Bruhat order. Section 6 applies this to the special element ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x). One first proves the result on the top subtraction-free cell R_{id,w_0}^{sf}, where Theorem 6.14 shows Δ_i^{±}(ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) dot{w}^{-1}) is subtraction-free. Then one specializes boundary variables to zero using a continuity lemma for subtraction-free functions, showing the same expressions remain subtraction-free on R_{v',w'}^{>0}. A contradiction using Zariski density and direct evaluation on R_{v,w}^{>0} rules out identically zero minors, so the relevant bottom-right principal generalized minors are strictly positive. By the Gaussian-decomposition criterion, this is exactly ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) dot{w}^{-1} in B B_-, equivalently ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) in B B_- dot{w}.

proof_techniques.name: Fomin-Shapiro atlas as an abstract local normal form
proof_techniques.description: The paper isolates a general structure sufficient for regularity: around every stratum Y_g there is an open star O_g and a diffeomorphism O_g ≅ (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × Z_g. The first factor is the stratum direction and the second is a normal slice stratified by all h ⪰ g. The positive part of the whole star must lie inside the chart, and the chart must identify each larger stratum with the product of the base stratum and a corresponding normal stratum. A smooth R_{>0}-action on Z_g supplies radial structure and allows links to be defined as unit-sphere sections.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the matching complex F_w, one could try to define O_g as the set of matchings containing a fixed partial resolution pattern and Z_g as the space of remaining crossing-resolution parameters. If every star of a cell is shown to split as cell × cone(link), the abstract theorem can replace ad hoc contraction arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Radialization by a norm-increasing cone action
proof_techniques.description: Instead of requiring an a priori PL cone, the paper assumes a smooth R_{>0}-action θ on a normal slice Z such that ||θ(t,x)|| strictly increases with t for x ≠ 0. This minimal analytic condition implies that every nonzero orbit meets the unit sphere exactly once and continuously. It yields an explicit homeomorphism Z_{≥0} ≅ Cone(Z_{≥0} ∩ S) by x ↦ (normalized point, radial parameter).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If crossing parameters in a matching cell can be scaled simultaneously or by a height/energy flow so that crossings move monotonically away from a base resolution, the same normalization could turn local deformation spaces into cones over links.

proof_techniques.name: Link induction through smaller links
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 3.12 uses a recursive local model: a point in a lower stratum of the link Lk_{g,h} has a neighborhood homeomorphic to Z_{p,h}^{≥0} × R^d, and Z_{p,h}^{≥0} is a cone on the smaller link Lk_{p,h}. Thus knowing smaller links are balls gives half-space neighborhoods in the current link. This proves the current link is a manifold with boundary; shellability identifies the boundary as a sphere; contractibility plus Poincare gives a ball.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is highly transferable to F_w if links of cells can be described in terms of links of larger cells after resolving one additional local feature. It suggests proving contractibility by a double induction on dimension of cells/links rather than by constructing a global deformation retraction.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary sphere from thin shellable face poset
proof_techniques.description: At each induction step, the boundary of a closed cell or link is already a regular CW complex by induction. Its face poset is an interval in the global closure poset. Because graded thin shellable posets have spherical order complexes, Theorem 3.9 turns this combinatorial boundary into an actual topological sphere. This avoids constructing explicit sphere homeomorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a natural strategy is to identify the face poset of a cell closure or link, prove its intervals are thin and shellable, and then invoke the same boundary-sphere mechanism. The crossing-resolution poset may have cubical or oriented-matroid-like shellings worth testing.

proof_techniques.name: Compact contractible manifold with spherical boundary implies ball
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly upgrades a compact contractible manifold with boundary to a closed ball using the generalized Poincare conjecture and Brown's Schoenflies theorem. Contractibility is obtained cheaply: once the space is a manifold with boundary whose interior is a contractible open cell, Brown collaring implies the whole manifold is homotopy equivalent to its interior.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If closures in F_w can be shown to be compact manifolds with boundary and their interiors are open balls or cubes, then one need not explicitly build ball coordinates. It suffices to prove the boundary is a sphere, likely by poset shellability.

proof_techniques.name: Local product projections from Gaussian or Bruhat decomposition
proof_techniques.description: In the affine flag variety, a big cell C_g admits two compatible factorizations of dot{g}U_-dot{g}^{-1} into U_1(g) and U_2(g). Comparing these factorizations gives a canonical isomorphism C_g ≅ opposite cell at g × Schubert cell at g, and this restricts to Richardson strata as R_h^f ∩ C_g ≅ R_g^f × R_h^g. This is the geometric origin of the Fomin-Shapiro product charts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One could look for an analogous factorization of crossing matchings into a part before a chosen cell and a residual part after it. The attaching-by-resolution construction already has a product-cube flavor, so a canonical projection to base cell and normal crossing data may be possible.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding into a larger stratified space to import charts
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not construct all local product charts directly inside G/P. Instead, it embeds an affine chart of G/P into an affine flag variety where Bruhat product charts are available. The map sends projected Richardson strata exactly to affine Richardson strata, so product structure in the target pulls back to the original space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, it may be fruitful to realize matchings or their resolution states inside a better-understood stratified object, such as a cubical complex, arc complex, noncrossing matching lattice, or rewriting/van Kampen diagram complex, where stars have standard product charts.

proof_techniques.name: Positivity or monotonicity to ensure charts contain full positive stars
proof_techniques.description: A key obstacle is that product charts may be defined only on open dense subsets. The paper proves the needed positive stars lie inside those domains using subtraction-free parametrizations: relevant functions are nonnegative on positive cells and cannot vanish identically on required strata, hence are strictly positive where needed. This verifies the Fomin-Shapiro axiom that Star_g^{≥0} is contained in O_g.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In F_w, local charts may fail on degenerate configurations such as tangencies or forbidden cancellations. An analogue would be to find monotone or sign-free invariants of quasi-reduced words/matchings that stay nonzero on all allowable cells in a star.

proof_techniques.name: Regular CW definition includes closure being a union of cells
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a version of regular CW complex requiring both that every closed cell is a closed ball whose open cell is its interior and that the closure of every cell is a union of cells. The second condition is essential for applying Bjorner's theorem identifying topology from the face poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, the attaching maps are defined cubically by crossing resolutions, but one still needs to verify that each cell closure is exactly a union of lower-dimensional matching cells and that attaching maps are embeddings if aiming for regularity rather than only contractibility.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that link induction is a known method, citing GLMS08 and Hersh14a, but that the difficult point is proving closed cells are topological manifolds; the Fomin-Shapiro atlas is designed precisely to overcome this. It also uses Bjorner's philosophy that, for regular CW complexes with suitable shellable face posets, topology is determined combinatorially. Remark 3.13 notes that the same machinery recovers Hersh's regularity theorem for links of the identity in totally nonnegative unipotent cells. Section 10 lists other settings where the authors expect similar regular CW structures, including Kac-Moody totally nonnegative flag varieties, double Bruhat cells, compactified electrical networks, Ising boundary correlation spaces, and amplituhedra/Grassmann polytopes; these are useful pointers for analogous conical-stratified regularity arguments.
why_relevant: Proves regular CW and ball-closure results for totally nonnegative cell decompositions. Its techniques may help show that crossing-resolution cell complexes have contractible cell closures or regular CW structures.
